input.userRequest: Recommend how Briefing Genie should summarize recovery work after a setup incident.

messages.content: You are Briefing Genie, a concise research briefing generator. Use only the provided synthetic source packet. Cite claims with source ids exactly as shown, such as A1 or B2. Do not invent citation ids, facts, or private data. Return a concise briefing suitable for a strategy review. Before writing each claim, verify that its full sentence is directly supported by its citations. Prefer the strongest direct citation for the decision point over a merely related citation. If a detail is only partially supported, narrow the claim or move the uncertainty to open questions. Avoid broad quantifiers, causal claims, and rollout recommendations unless the cited source directly supports them.

User request: Recommend how Briefing Genie should summarize recovery work after a setup incident.

Source packet: Incident Recovery Communication Packet

Packet summary: Support, status-page, and customer-success notes about communicating recovery after failed installs.

Sources:

[S1] Support incident recap
Type: support recap
A failed installer release caused 31 setup tickets in two days. The highest-friction users had partial installs: they could not tell whether to rerun setup, clear credentials, or wait for a patched CLI.

The support recap lists partial installs as the biggest source of confusion. Users were unsure whether rerunning setup would corrupt local state or clear credentials. Agents asked for a recovery message that starts with symptoms and then gives one safe next action.

[S2] Status page draft
Type: status draft
The draft status update says the issue is resolved, but engineering has only verified recovery on macOS and Linux. Windows recovery steps are still being validated by the tooling team.

The status draft was written before Windows recovery validation finished. Engineering confirmed macOS and Linux, but the Windows path still had an unresolved credential-cache issue. The draft's word 'resolved' was flagged as too broad unless the environment caveat stayed visible.

[S3] Customer success guidance
Type: customer guidance
Customer success wants a concise message that separates who is affected, what action they should take, and which environments are still pending validation. They specifically asked not to claim universal resolution until Windows is verified.

Customer success asked for a message that separates affected users, recommended action, and pending validation. They wanted the Windows caveat in the first paragraph rather than a footnote. Their concern was that customers would forward the status update internally as proof of universal recovery.

[S4] Engineering fix note
Type: engineering note
The patched CLI adds a recovery command that checks stale credentials and cleans partial install state. The command is available in the release candidate, but docs screenshots still show the old installer flow.

The engineering note says the recovery command handles stale credentials and partial install cleanup. The command is available in the release candidate, while screenshots and public docs still show the old installer path. Engineering asked docs to update before broad promotion.

[S5] Docs update checklist
Type: docs checklist
The docs update checklist requires the new recovery command, credential cleanup instructions, and screenshots for the patched installer path. The docs owner marked Windows screenshots as pending. The checklist says customer communication should not point users to the old installer flow once the release candidate is promoted.

[S6] Support macro draft
Type: support macro
The support macro draft separates users into verified macOS, verified Linux, and pending Windows recovery paths. It gives partial-install users one safe command and tells Windows users that validation is still in progress. Support asked for the same segmentation to appear in any generated incident briefing.

output.title: How to summarize recovery work after the setup incident
output.summary: Summarize recovery in a customer-forward way that reduces confusion for partial installs by leading with symptoms, giving one safe next action, and clearly segmenting guidance by environment—while avoiding claims of universal resolution until Windows recovery is validated.

claims.text: Lead the recovery message with symptoms (especially partial-install indicators) and then provide one safe next action, because partial installs were the biggest source of confusion and users were unsure whether rerunning setup would corrupt local state or clear credentials.

Avoid stating the incident is fully “resolved” without an explicit environment caveat, because engineering has only verified recovery on macOS and Linux while Windows recovery steps are still being validated due to an unresolved credential-cache issue.

Structure the summary to clearly separate (1) who is affected, (2) what action they should take, and (3) which environments remain pending validation, and put the Windows caveat in the first paragraph rather than as a footnote.
Include the existence and purpose of the patched CLI recovery command (it checks stale credentials and cleans partial install state), and note that it is available in the release candidate.

Do not direct users to the old installer flow once the release candidate is promoted, because the docs checklist requires updated recovery-command instructions and a patched installer path, with Windows screenshots still pending.

openQuestions: Has Windows recovery validation completed, and if not, what is the current recommended workaround for the credential-cache issue?
When will documentation (including Windows screenshots) be updated so communications can safely reference the new recovery flow?
What exact “one safe command” should be standardized in the recovery summary for partial-install users, and does it differ by OS?
Should the external status-page wording be updated immediately to reflect macOS/Linux verification and Windows pending validation, and what approval is required?
output.recommendation: Use a segmented recovery summary format: start with the top symptoms (call out partial installs), give a single safe next action centered on the patched CLI recovery command, and then clearly label OS-specific status as “verified” for macOS/Linux and “pending validation” for Windows—avoiding any universal “resolved” language until Windows is verified and the docs/screenshots are updated so you don’t point users to the old installer flow.
